464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: No documented first-touch to first-class journey (discovery call → payment → induction → buddy assignment → first class → check-in at day 3/7)
- **Churn & win-back**: No process for pause requests, cancellations, exit interviews, or re-engagement campaigns
- **Coaching standards & quality**: No audit checklist, class observation protocol, or feedback loops tied to member outcomes
- **Member retention touchpoints**: No scheduled check-ins, milestone tracking, or 30-day goal follow-ups
- **Referral programme**: No mechanics, incentive structure, or tracking workflow
- **Supply chain**: No SOP for ordering, receiving, inventory thresholds, or vendor management
- **Complaints & incidents**: No escalation path, documentation, or resolution timeline
- **Data governance**: No GDPR/privacy audit trail, consent capture, or data retention policy
- **Financial controls**: No cash handling, payment disputes, refund authorization, or reconciliation process
- **Health & safety incident reporting**: No form, reporting line, or corrective action tracking

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coaches (20/24)**: Missing coaching development roadmap, skill assessment framework, and performance feedback cadence; class observation currently informal
- **Clients (23/33)**: No outcomes tracking, transformation pillar definition, or quarterly program launch comms; member data privacy missing entirely
- **Weekly Key Coach Tasks**: Not documented—likely ad hoc; needs dependencies mapped (prep, delivery, feedback, next-week planning)
- **Facilities (5/6)**: Likely missing deep-clean schedules, equipment maintenance logs, and emergency protocols
- **Marketing (5/5)**: Likely missing referral programme integration, member success story capture, or paid ad SOP

---

# **Build next**

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE — THE FORGE

## REVENUE — STOP THE SLIDE & CAPITALISE ON CAPACITY

• **Churn is your killer: 5.74% in May, 0.00% in June is excellent, but Q2 average (May–June: 2.87%) is still 50% higher than Q1 (Nov–Jan: 1.33%). At 122–147 members, you have the space; you're losing them. Retention audit required — survey last 20 leavers, fix the friction (class times, coaching quality, facilities), and target 1.5% monthly churn. Every 1% saved = £180–200 recurring revenue.**

• **Average Revenue per Member (AR/M) collapsed: £170.43 (December) → £120.48 (June). This is your biggest red flag. You've added 23 net members in 6 months but lost £50 per member revenue. Diagnose now: Are new members on discounted rates? Loyalty plan leakage? Run an upsell blitz — PT packages, nutrition coaching, merchandise — target £150 AR/M by August, worth £3,300+ monthly.**

• **June revenue dropped 12.3% month-on-month (£20,198 to £17,710) despite flat active clients (122→147). Two months post-relocation, you should be *growing*, not declining. Capacity is there — fill quiet class slots with off-peak pricing (e.g., 9am weekday classes at £4/drop-in vs standard £6), partner with local employers for lunchtime blocks.**

• **December was your peak (£21,133, 49.54% margin) — likely New Year Resolution influx *and* pricing power. Replicate: run a summer body campaign (July–Aug), lock in 6-month contracts at a 10% uplift, and add a 'VIP tier' (£45/month, includes 2 PT sessions) to segment the membership and boost AR/M.**

---

## EXPENSES — REIN IN NON-PEOPLE COSTS

• **Non-people expenses are out of control in Q2 (May: £9,233, June: £9,390) vs Q1 (Nov: £4,303, Dec: £5,651, Jan: £8,165). Excluding April's one-off fit-out, you're running £5,000–9,000 monthly on utilities, rent, insurance, equipment, supplies. Breakdown required: Is June's £9,390 a new baseline post-relocation (larger rent

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo can take Build: Lower and Strongevity (aligns with her strength); Eilis takes Build: Upper and Elevate (matches her current load and skill level).

• **Move Grace's PT and No Sweat Intros to JoJo:** Her 3 PT clients and 3 No Sweat Intros should transfer to JoJo, who has the experience and rapport. This formalises JoJo as lead coach for new member onboarding.

• **Reassign Body Composition Scans away from coaches:** All 29 scans (Grace's 9 + JoJo's 20) should be handled by reception or a junior staff member—they're admin, not coaching. This frees ~6 hours monthly from JoJo's load.

• **Balance JoJo's PT portfolio:** After removing scans, JoJo will have ~57 billable/class sessions (44 PT + new transfers). Monitor her weekly schedule to cap PT at 8–10 per week so she has energy for classes and leadership.

• **Expand Eilis's PT gradually:** She's currently underused on PT (11 sessions). Once Grace's classes move to her, trial Eilis for 2–3 of Grace's PT clients (lower-complexity, newer members) to build her confidence and revenue per coach.

• **Plug the Circuits gap:** JoJo is the only coach delivering Circuits (6 sessions). Train Eilis on Circuits as her confidence grows, or rotate it to a lower priority if demand is soft.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time between sessions:**

1. **Launch a paid "Open Gym + Coach" block on Monday/Thursday afternoons** — staff one coach to float between 2-3pm when studios are empty, charge £8-12 per session for members wanting unsupervised lifting with form checks available. Fills dead time and captures revenue from your Build: Upper devotees who want extra volume.

2. **Add a 30-min "Lift + Mobility" workshop slot Saturday mornings before the quietest day's classes start** — bridge Mobility's proven appeal (225 attendees) with a lower-barrier entry to Build classes; position it as a prep session to reduce perceived intensity of your least popular strength classes.

**To lift the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Engine or Capacity to Tuesday/Wednesday evenings and run a 4-week "Try for Free" trial with direct invites to Build: Upper members** — your attendance data shows Wed/Tues are peak, and Build attendees are already comfortable with structured strength work; reframe these as "conditioning finishers" rather than standalone classes to lower the intimidation factor driving low uptake.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue reality, not your dreams**
Audit what your coaches are *actually* selling right now (group classes, PT sessions, nutrition plans, mobility work). Don't design a menu of what you wish you offered. Ask your top 3 coaches: "What gets booked most? What do members ask for but we don't have?" This becomes your foundation.

**2. Price by value extracted, not time spent**
Don't charge £30/hour for coaching because that's "market rate." Ask: *What's the transformation worth?* A 12-week body composition program might be £600 because it's results-based. A drop-in mobility class might be £12 because it's convenience. Make coaches understand the difference—they'll sell with conviction.

**3. Create TWO different menus with ruthless clarity**
- **Member-facing**: Clean, benefit-led copy ("Build Strength" not "3x/week barbell training"). Pricing, what's included (e.g., form check, nutrition guidance), how to book.
- **Coach-facing**: The operational spec—what's *in* each service (e.g., "PT session = 1:1 assessment, program design, video library access"). Prevents coaches making up their own rules.

**4. Test pricing on 5 existing members in conversation**
Don't guess. Ring 5 loyal members, ask: "If we offered 8-week transformation packages at £400, would you buy?" Their hesitation or enthusiasm tells you everything. Adjust before launch.

**5. Publish it Friday, measure bookings by next Friday**
Get it live on your website and in

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

1. **You've cracked cost-per-lead efficiency but broken conversion.** July delivered 165 leads at £2/lead—a 92% drop in cost versus June and a 10x improvement on April. But close rate collapsed from 68% (Apr) to 4% (Jul). You're buying cheap volume from the wrong people. The leads are either unqualified, cold-intent, or your follow-up process has fallen apart.

2. **Your April baseline was your sweet spot: 20 sales for £435 spend.** You're now spending less (£376) but getting fewer sales (7 vs 20). Revenue per sale tells the real story—April returned £66.50 per pound spent; July returns £3.54. You've optimized for clicks, not conversions.

3. **May-to-June showed warning signs you ignored.** CPL doubled month-on-month while close rate stayed high(ish). That signalled audience fatigue and creep toward lower-intent traffic. July's 8x CPL drop suggests the algorithm found a completely different audience—likely bargain-hunters, gym-curious randoms, or people in wrong locations/life stages.

**Actions**

1. **Pause all July-winning audiences and creatives immediately.** Those 165 leads aren't buyers. Audit your follow-up: are replies being answered same-day? Is there a qualification call? If your process is sound, this audience is just poor fit. Revert to April targeting: reuse that exact audience segment, location radius, age range, and interest stack. Run a small £80/week test to confirm it still converts.

2. **Test three narrow intent segments next week.** Create separate ad sets for: (a) "Local women fitness" + "weight loss" + exclude under-25s, (b) newly engaged/married women (life event targeting), (c) existing gym members (lookalike). Keep CPL cap around £4–6 based on April's 20-sales math. Lower CPL targets are pulling in noise.

3. **Change creative immediately.** July's ads clearly spoke to curiosity, not commitment. Film a 15-second testimonial from someone who actually completed the 6

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE: ARREST THE DECLINE & REBUILD MEMBER VALUE

- **Rev/member has collapsed from £138 (Apr) to £115 (Jun) — a 17% drop in 8 weeks.** This isn't churn alone; it's pricing or ancillary sales leakage. Check: are members downgrading tiers post-relocation? Are class packs, PT blocks, or merchandise sales tracking? You should target £135+ per member by July. Audit your pricing tier uptake now — if the bigger studio hasn't lifted premium memberships or upsells, that's the fast win.

- **Total revenue is volatile but flat: last 3 months average £15,879 vs prior 3 months £15,524 (+ 2% only).** Member count grew 16 members (122→137) but revenue stayed dead. You've added capacity at the new studio — use it: run member surveys on class scheduling, introduce off-peak discounts (e.g. 10% off 10am slots) to flatten attendance curves and drive frequency (which drives stickiness and upsell).

- **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May post-move; dropped to 3.3% in June — good stabilisation, but 3.3% monthly is 39% annualised.** Target ≤2% monthly. Implement 30-day post-relocation onboarding calls and a "welcome-back" offer (free class + protein shake) for lapsed members. The bigger studio should reduce churn via better class variety.

- **Cash collection timing is healthy (revenue reasonably tracks bank deposits), but June net cash is -£2,521.** You're not in distress (April relocation absorbed), but you're not building reserves. Once revenue stabilises at £16.5k+, you should clear £4k–£6k monthly; currently you're at break-even. See expenses below.

---

## EXPENSES: IDENTIFY THE JUNE OVERSPEND

- **June cash out (£20,644) vs June revenue (£15,765) is unsustainable — you're spending 131% of revenue.** Last 3 months: out averaged £19,864; prior 3 months: £19,544 (flat, high). Breakout Q: is June's £20.6k driven by utilities, staffing, or stock for the bigger studio? If staffing: you may have over-hired post-move. If utilities: the new space is more expensive than planned — renegotiate rates now. If stock/inventory: t
